LC #102 — Binary Tree Level Order Traversal medium

Trees Pattern  |  Day 8 Study Material

📋 Problem + Core Insight

🌳 LeetCode 102: Binary Tree Level Order Traversal

The Core Insight: BFS processes nodes level by level naturally.

The trick: before the inner loop, snapshot level_size = len(queue).

This freezes the count for the current level — children added during this level

are counted separately next iteration.

📋 Problem

Given the root of a binary tree, return its level-order traversal as a list of lists —

each inner list contains the values at that depth.

Input:  [3, 9, 20, null, null, 15, 7]
Output: [[3], [9, 20], [15, 7]]

Constraints: 0 ≤ nodes ≤ 2000 | -1000 ≤ Node.val ≤ 1000

🧠 Mental Models

🧠 Choose Your Mental Model

Model The "Story" Mechanism
Floor-by-Floor Inspection "I inspect a building one floor at a time. Before I start a floor, I count how many doors (nodes) are on it — that count comes from how many doors I added to my list when I finished the floor above." level_size = len(queue) before inner loop
Wave Propagation "Dropping a stone in water. Each ripple ring = one level. Process all nodes in the current ring before expanding the next ring." Queue grows outward, level boundary = len(queue) snapshot
DFS + Depth Tag "Visit every node, tag it with its depth. Group by depth tag at the end." Slower O(n log n), but shows the relationship between DFS depth and BFS levels

📊 Complexity Analysis

🔬 Complexity Analysis

The Core Insight: BFS is O(n) because it processes each node exactly once.

The DFS approach is O(n log n) because it sorts all (depth, val) pairs — unnecessary extra cost

when BFS naturally delivers nodes in level order.

📊 Why BFS is Natural for Level Order

After sorting by depth (brute force), each node at depth d was already in level-order

within that depth IF we used left-first DFS. But we still pay O(n log n) for the sort.

BFS avoids this: the queue maintains left-to-right order within each level automatically.

Approach Time Space Order guarantee
DFS + sort O(n log n) O(n) Sort-dependent
BFS (optimal) O(n) O(n) Natural left-to-right

The Level Snapshot Trick

Queue at start:      [3]
After level 1:       [9, 20]     → level_size was 1, result = [[3]]
After level 2:       [15, 7]     → level_size was 2, result = [[3],[9,20]]
After level 3:       []          → level_size was 2, result = [[3],[9,20],[15,7]]

level_size = len(queue) freezes the count BEFORE the inner loop adds children.

✅ Key Rule: Snapshot len(queue) before the inner loop.

This is the entire secret to BFS level separation.

🚀 Optimal Solution

from collections import deque

def levelOrder(root: TreeNode) -> list[list[int]]:
    """
    BFS with level snapshot.
    Time: O(n)  |  Space: O(n) — queue holds at most one full level
    The critical line: level_size = len(queue) BEFORE the inner loop.
    """
    if not root:
        return []

    result = []
    queue = deque([root])

    while queue:
        level_size = len(queue)     # ← snapshot: nodes at THIS level
        level = []

        for _ in range(level_size):
            node = queue.popleft()
            level.append(node.val)

            if node.left:  queue.append(node.left)
            if node.right: queue.append(node.right)

        result.append(level)

    return result


# Tests
root = build_tree([3, 9, 20, None, None, 15, 7])
print("Level order:", levelOrder(root))   # [[3], [9, 20], [15, 7]]

root2 = build_tree([1])
print("Single node:", levelOrder(root2))  # [[1]]

print("Empty:", levelOrder(None))         # []

# Extension: return values in reverse level order (bottom-up)
# Just return result[::-1]
print("Reversed:", levelOrder(root)[::-1])  # [[15, 7], [9, 20], [3]]

🎤 Interview Q&A

🎤 5 Interview Q&A

Q1: What is the key to separating levels in BFS?

Answer: level_size = len(queue) — snapshot the queue size BEFORE the inner loop.

This tells you exactly how many nodes belong to the current level.

Children added during this loop are at the next level and get counted in the next iteration.

Without this snapshot, you cannot distinguish where one level ends and the next begins.

Q2: What is the space complexity and what determines it?

Answer: O(n) — the queue holds at most one full level at a time.

The widest level of a perfect binary tree of depth h contains n/2 nodes (the leaf level).

So worst-case queue size = O(n/2) = O(n). The result list also takes O(n) total.

Q3: BFS vs DFS for level order — when would you choose DFS?

Answer: In languages without a built-in queue (rare). Or when recursion is required

by the problem's API (e.g., a tree visitor pattern). The DFS approach needs depth tracking

and produces O(n log n) due to sorting. In practice: always use BFS for level order.

Q4: How do you modify this to get the RIGHT side view (LC #199)?

Answer: Capture only the LAST element of each level:

if i == level_size - 1:
    result.append(node.val)

Or keep the existing structure and return [level[-1] for level in levelOrder(root)].

Q5: Real-world use case for level-order traversal?

Answer: Many. Tree serialization (the format LeetCode uses is level-order).

Finding the minimum depth (first level with a leaf node).

Printing an org chart or category hierarchy tier by tier.

At Citi: processing all servers in the same network tier together before moving to the next tier.

Any "breadth-first" problem: shortest path in an unweighted graph is BFS.

💼 The Citi Angle

💼 The Citi Narrative

Context: Citi's server fleet was organized in a hierarchy: Region → DataCenter → Rack → Server.

This formed a tree structure. Monthly capacity reviews processed servers tier-by-tier:

first all regions, then all datacenters within each region, then racks, then individual servers.

How Level Order Applied: The capacity review report was essentially a level-order traversal

of the infrastructure tree. Each "level" in the report corresponded to a tier in the hierarchy.

Processing level-by-level enabled parallel workload distribution: all servers at the same tier

could be processed simultaneously (no dependency on each other).

Practical Extension: Instead of collecting values, the algorithm collected (server_id, avg_cpu)

pairs per tier. If any tier had average CPU above 80%, the review flagged that tier before

drilling into the individual server level.

SQL Equivalent: Level-order traversal of hierarchical data in SQL is a recursive CTE.

The "level" in BFS corresponds to the depth column in a recursive CTE:

WITH RECURSIVE hierarchy AS (
    SELECT id, name, parent_id, 0 AS depth FROM infrastructure WHERE parent_id IS NULL
    UNION ALL
    SELECT c.id, c.name, c.parent_id, h.depth + 1
    FROM infrastructure c JOIN hierarchy h ON c.parent_id = h.id
)
SELECT depth, name FROM hierarchy ORDER BY depth, name;

Interview Line: *"At Citi, the infrastructure hierarchy was a tree. Monthly reviews

processed it level-by-level — all datacenters first, then racks, then servers.

That's exactly level-order traversal. The BFS queue made it natural:

snapshot level size, process that tier, then the next."*

🎯 Summary

🎯 Summary: Key Takeaways

The Pattern

BFS + Level Snapshot: level_size = len(queue) before the inner loop.

The One Rule

Process exactly level_size nodes in the inner loop. The children they enqueue belong to the NEXT level.

Common Variants (all use the same skeleton)

Problem Change
Level order (this) Collect full level
Right side view Take level[-1] only
Zigzag level order Alternate append/appendleft
Average of levels sum(level) / len(level)
Max width max(max_w, level_size)
Minimum depth Stop at first level with a leaf

Complexity

Approach Time Space
DFS + sort O(n log n) O(n)
BFS (optimal) O(n) O(n)

Interview Confidence Checklist

  • ☐ Can write BFS level order from memory in 4 minutes
  • ☐ Can explain why level_size = len(queue) BEFORE the loop
  • ☐ Can name 3 variants that use the same skeleton
  • ☐ Can connect to Citi: tier-by-tier infrastructure processing

"Simplicity and clarity is Gold." — Sean's Study Mantra 🚀
